info.name: Auth – Session (POST /api/v1/auth/refresh + POST /api/v1/auth/logout)
info.description:
E2E tests for POST /api/v1/auth/refresh and POST /api/v1/auth/logout.

Folder execution order (orchestrated by make e2e-auth):
  1. happy-path    — setup user + login two devices; assert refresh cookie attrs; logout + cross-endpoint blocklist check; sibling session survives
  2. failures      — present a token revoked via logout → 401 + cookie cleared in real HTTP response
  3. token-reuse   — rotate once, reuse old token → 401 token_reuse_detected; confirm family kill (successor also dead)
  4. rate-limiting — refresh IP limiter (5 warmups + 6th → 429); logout IP limiter (5 warmups + 6th → 429)

Cookie management: Postman's cookie jar is not used. The refresh_token value is
extracted from the raw Set-Cookie header and stored in collection variables; all
requests that need the cookie send it via an explicit Cookie: header.

Run `make _e2e-db-clean` and `make _e2e-kv-clean` before this collection.
Requires TRUSTED_PROXIES=127.0.0.1/32 in .env.

Notes on what is NOT covered here (already covered by internal tests):
  POST /refresh:
  - Missing cookie → 401 missing_token                — handler_test.go TestRefresh_MissingCookie
  - Expired / malformed JWT → 401 invalid_token        — handler_test.go TestRefresh_MalformedToken_Returns401
  - JTI not found in DB → 401 invalid_token            — service_test.go + store_test.go integration
  - DB expires_at in the past → 401 invalid_token      — service_test.go
  - Locked account → 423 + cookie cleared              — handler_test.go TestRefresh_ErrAccountLocked_423_CookieCleared
  - Inactive account → 403 + cookie cleared            — handler_test.go TestRefresh_ErrAccountInactive_403_CookieCleared
  - User row deleted → 401                             — service_test.go
  - last_active_at / last_login_at stamped             — store_test.go TestStore_RotateRefreshTokenTx_Integration
  - token_refreshed audit row                          — store_test.go same
  - token_family_revoked audit row                     — store_test.go TestStore_RevokeFamilyTokens_Integration
  - Concurrent rotation → exactly one 200, one 401     — store_test.go TestStore_RotateRefreshTokenTx_ConcurrentRotation_Integration
  POST /logout:
  - Missing cookie → 204                              — handler_test.go TestLogout_MissingCookie_Returns204
  - Malformed / expired cookie → 204, cookie cleared  — handler_test.go TestLogout_MalformedCookie_Returns204_CookieCleared
  - No Authorization header → 204                     — handler design (best-effort parse)
  - Expired access token → 204, blocklist NOT called  — handler_test.go TestLogout_ExpiredAccessToken_BlocklistNotCalled
  - Malformed Bearer → 204, blocklist NOT called      — handler_test.go TestLogout_MalformedAccessToken_BlocklistNotCalled
  - Double logout → 204 both times                    — store_test.go LogoutTx idempotent test
  - KV store failure → 204                            — handler_test.go TestLogout_NilBlocklist_ValidAccessToken_NoError
  - Session ended_at set in DB                        — store_test.go TestStore_LogoutTx_Integration
  - logout audit row written                          — store_test.go same
  - Access token JTI blocklisted with correct TTL     — handler_test.go TestLogout_ValidAccessToken_BlocklistCalled
  - Refresh token revoked in DB with reason='logout'  — store_test.go same

exec: // Increment the request sequence counter and derive a unique X-Forwarded-For IP
// for every request so non-RL folders never exhaust any rate-limit bucket.
// The rate-limiting folder overrides _xff to 127.0.0.1 via its own prerequest.

item.name: happy-path
item.description:
Registers the gmail_test_email user, fetches the OTP from Gmail, verifies the email, and logs in on two separate devices (A and B). Each login creates an independent token family.

Refresh assertions: POST /refresh for device A returns 200 with a new access_token in the JSON body and a new refresh_token Set-Cookie header bearing the required security attributes (HttpOnly, SameSite=Strict, Path=/api/v1/auth, positive Max-Age). These attributes are enforced by the real HTTP layer and cannot be verified by handler unit tests alone.

Logout assertions: POST /logout for device A returns 204 with an empty body and a Set-Cookie that clears the refresh_token (MaxAge=-1). A subsequent GET /me with the revoked access token returns 401 — this cross-endpoint check verifies that the KV blocklist is wired into the auth middleware used by /me. Device B is then refreshed to confirm that logging out device A did not kill the sibling session.

item.name: POST /register → 201 (setup user)

exec: // Use the real Gmail address so the verification OTP lands in the inbox
// that gmail_access_token can read. The address is cleaned by _e2e-db-clean
// before each run so this register never 409 on re-runs.
console.error('[ERROR] gmail_test_email not set in environment — add it to environment.json.');
// Record the timestamp just before the register request fires so the
// Gmail query can use after:<ts> to exclude older emails from prior runs.
// Placed in the test script (not prerequest) because pm.sendRequest is async.
// The callback executes during the --delay-request idle window before the
// next request fires, so 'sss_code' is set in time.
if (!token) { console.warn('[WARN] gmail_access_token not set — skipping Gmail fetch'); return; }
console.log('[INFO] Waiting 8 s for verification email to arrive in Gmail...');
console.log('[INFO] Done waiting. Querying Gmail API (async)...');
// Use after:<unix-ts> so only emails received during THIS run are matched.
// newer_than:1h would also match OTPs from previous runs within the same hour.
const query = encodeURIComponent('subject:"Verify your" after:' + regTs);
if (err) { console.error('[ERROR] Gmail list failed:', err); return; }
if (res.code === 401) { console.error('[ERROR] Gmail token expired — refresh at https://developers.google.com/oauthplayground'); return; }
if (!msgs || !msgs.length) { console.warn('[WARN] No matching email found in Gmail.'); return; }
if (err2) { console.error('[ERROR] Gmail fetch failed:', err2); return; }
const p = parts.find(x => x.mimeType === 'text/plain') || parts.find(x => x.mimeType === 'text/html');
if (!body) { console.warn('[WARN] Could not decode email body.'); return; }
if (!match) { console.warn('[WARN] No 6-digit OTP found in email body.'); return; }
console.log('[INFO] OTP stored in collection variable "sss_code":', match[1]);

item.name: POST /verify-email → 200 (consume OTP)

exec: // Validate that the Gmail fetch in the register test script completed.
console.error('[ERROR] collection variable "sss_code" is empty — Gmail fetch did not complete.');
console.error('        Check: gmail_access_token is set and not expired, --delay-request >= 8000.');

item.name: POST /login device A → 200 (store tokens)

exec: // Extract the raw refresh_token cookie value from Set-Cookie.
// pm.response.cookies.get() cannot read HttpOnly attributes so we parse
// the header directly. We also store it for manual Cookie: injection.
console.log('[INFO] Device A refresh token stored.');
console.error('[ERROR] Could not extract refresh_token from Set-Cookie.');

item.name: POST /login device B → 200 (sibling session, separate family_id)

exec: // A second login creates a new token family in the DB (new family_id).
// This device is used at the end of happy-path to prove that
// logging out device A does not revoke device B's session.
pm.test('refresh_token cookie present for device B', () => pm.expect(sc).to.include('refresh_token='));
console.log('[INFO] Device B refresh token stored.');
console.error('[ERROR] Could not extract device B refresh_token from Set-Cookie.');

item.name: POST /refresh device A → 200 (assert cookie attrs, store new tokens)

exec: // New access_token must be present in the JSON body.
// These are the E2E-critical checks: they verify that the real HTTP stack
// (not just handler logic) sets the correct security attributes on the
// replacement refresh_token cookie. pm.response.cookies.get() cannot read
// HttpOnly or SameSite, so we parse the raw Set-Cookie header.
pm.test('new refresh_token cookie present in Set-Cookie', () => {
pm.test('refresh_token cookie has positive Max-Age (derived from DB expires_at)', () => {
// Store old + new tokens for subsequent steps.
// sss_old_refresh_token_a is used by token-reuse folder.
console.error('[ERROR] Could not extract new refresh_token from Set-Cookie.');

item.name: POST /logout device A → 204 (cookie cleared in Set-Cookie)

exec: // The refresh_token cookie must be cleared via Max-Age=-1 in the real HTTP
// response. This attribute combination is enforced by the full HTTP stack.
pm.test('Set-Cookie header present (cookie is being cleared)', () => pm.expect(sc).to.not.be.empty);
// net/http serialises this as Max-Age=0 in the Set-Cookie header.

item.name: GET /me with old access_token_a → 401 (blocklisted by KV store)

exec: // This is the critical cross-endpoint check: after logout, the same access token
// that was used moments ago must be rejected by GET /me. The blocklist entry
// is written by the logout handler (via context.WithoutCancel so it survives
// request cancellation — ADR-004) and read by the auth middleware that guards /me.
// Neither step can be verified by a unit test for a single handler.
pm.test('status is 401 (access token blocklisted after logout)', () => pm.response.to.have.status(401));
pm.test('code indicates token is invalid or revoked', () => {

item.name: POST /refresh device B → 200 (sibling session unaffected by device A logout)

exec: // POST /logout only revokes the presenting device's token family.
// (LogoutTx sets revoked_at on the one refresh_token row matching the JTI;
// it does NOT call RevokeFamilyTokensTx.) Device B's family must still be live.
pm.test('status is 200 (device B refresh unaffected by device A logout)', () => pm.response.to.have.status(200));

item.name: failures
item.description:
Verifies that a refresh token revoked via logout (revoke_reason='logout') is rejected with the correct HTTP response: 401 status and a Set-Cookie that clears the cookie in the real HTTP response.

All other failure modes (missing cookie, expired JWT, locked/inactive account, etc.) are covered by internal tests — see the collection description for the full list with function references.

Setup: logs in a dedicated device C so this folder is self-contained and does not depend on state from happy-path.

item.name: POST /login device C → 200 (setup for failures folder)

exec: pm.test('status is 200 (device C logged in)', () => pm.response.to.have.status(200));
console.error('[ERROR] Could not extract device C refresh_token.');

item.name: POST /logout device C → 204 (revoke device C token with reason='logout')

exec: pm.test('status is 204 (device C logged out)', () => pm.response.to.have.status(204));

item.name: POST /refresh with revoked token (device C) → 401, cookie cleared

exec: // The token was revoked with reason='logout'. The service checks revoked_at IS NOT NULL
// on the token row regardless of the revoke reason, so it returns token_reuse_detected
// and calls RevokeFamilyTokensTx just as it would for a rotated token. The handler
// clears the cookie on any refresh error.
// This is an E2E-only check: it verifies the real HTTP Set-Cookie clear
// attribute on an error path (internal tests only check the status code).
// Cookie must be cleared: the handler calls clearRefreshCookie on all error paths.
pm.test('Set-Cookie header clears the refresh_token cookie on error', () => {

item.name: token-reuse
item.description:
Verifies the RFC 6819 / ADR-011 token reuse detection and family kill sequence:
  1. Login a fresh device D → valid refresh token D.
  2. POST /refresh with D → 200, token E issued; D is now revoked with reason='rotated'.
  3. POST /refresh with old token D → 401 token_reuse_detected + cookie cleared.
     (RotateRefreshTokenTx detects revoked_at IS NOT NULL and calls RevokeFamilyTokensTx.)
  4. POST /refresh with token E (the legitimate successor, now family-killed) → 401 invalid_token.
     Re-authentication is required after family revocation.

Only this multi-request sequence can validate the DB state transition across all three steps. Individual steps are covered by store_test.go integration tests; the E2E value is the orchestrated chain using real JWT secrets and a live DB.

item.name: POST /login device D → 200 (fresh session for reuse chain)

exec: pm.test('status is 200 (device D logged in)', () => pm.response.to.have.status(200));
console.log('[INFO] Device D (original) refresh token stored.');
console.error('[ERROR] Could not extract device D refresh_token.');

item.name: POST /refresh device D → 200 (rotate: D consumed, E issued)

exec: // Legitimate first rotation. Token D is now revoked with reason='rotated'.
// Token E (the successor) is issued and stored. The next request will reuse D.
console.log('[INFO] Token E (successor) stored. Reuse of token D will follow.');
console.error('[ERROR] Could not extract successor refresh_token (E).');

item.name: POST /refresh with old token D (revoked) → 401 token_reuse_detected, cookie cleared

exec: // Token D has revoked_at IS NOT NULL (reason='rotated').
// RotateRefreshTokenTx detects this and calls RevokeFamilyTokensTx,
// which also revokes token E. The handler then returns token_reuse_detected.
// Cookie must be cleared in the real HTTP response.
pm.test('refresh_token cookie cleared on reuse detection', () => {

item.name: POST /refresh with token E (family-killed successor) → 401 token_reuse_detected

exec: // RevokeFamilyTokensTx was called in the previous step. Token E was revoked
// even though it was never presented — it is now dead because its family_id
// was targeted. When E is presented the service sees revoked_at IS NOT NULL
// and returns token_reuse_detected (the same path as any revoked token).
// The user must re-authenticate to get a new session.
pm.test('status is 401 (family-killed successor also rejected)', () => pm.response.to.have.status(401));
pm.test('code is token_reuse_detected (service treats any revoked token the same way)', () => {

item.name: rate-limiting
item.description:
Verifies both rate limiters by pinning all requests to a single IP (127.0.0.1).
Requires Redis flushed (make _e2e-kv-clean) before this folder runs — enforced by make e2e-auth.

POST /refresh — rfsh:ip: limiter: burst=5, rate=5/15min, key prefix rfsh:ip:
  Warmup 1–5 consume the bucket. The 6th request returns 429 too_many_requests.
  Retry-After = ceil(1 / (5/900)) = 180 s.
  Warmup requests carry no valid cookie — the rate limiter is middleware that
  fires before token validation, so any request triggers a slot deduction.

POST /logout — lgout:ip: limiter: burst=5, rate=5/1min, key prefix lgout:ip:
  Warmup 1–5 consume the bucket. The 6th request returns 429 too_many_requests.
  Retry-After = ceil(1 / (5/60)) = 12 s.
  Warmup requests carry no valid cookie — logout returns 204 for missing/invalid
  tokens (best-effort design) so the rate limiter slot still fires.

The two limiters use different bucket prefixes (rfsh:ip: vs lgout:ip:) and do not
interfere with each other within the same folder run.

exec: // Override the collection-level unique IP so every request in this
// folder shares the 127.0.0.1 bucket and exhausts it together.

item.name: refresh warmup 1/5 — consume rfsh:ip: slot (→ not 429)
refresh warmup 2/5 — consume rfsh:ip: slot (→ not 429)
refresh warmup 3/5 — consume rfsh:ip: slot (→ not 429)
refresh warmup 4/5 — consume rfsh:ip: slot (→ not 429)
refresh warmup 5/5 — consume rfsh:ip: slot (→ not 429, last allowed)

exec: pm.test('not rate-limited yet (rfsh:ip: slot 5 — last allowed)', () => pm.expect(pm.response.code).to.not.eql(429));

item.name: refresh 6th request → 429 (rfsh:ip: bucket exhausted)
logout warmup 1/5 — consume lgout:ip: slot (→ not 429)
logout warmup 2/5 — consume lgout:ip: slot (→ not 429)
logout warmup 3/5 — consume lgout:ip: slot (→ not 429)
logout warmup 4/5 — consume lgout:ip: slot (→ not 429)
logout warmup 5/5 — consume lgout:ip: slot (→ not 429, last allowed)

exec: pm.test('not rate-limited yet (lgout:ip: slot 5/5 — last allowed)', () => pm.expect(pm.response.code).to.not.eql(429));

item.name: logout 6th request → 429 (lgout:ip: bucket exhausted)

variable.description: Auto-incremented counter used by the collection prerequest to generate unique X-Forwarded-For IPs.
Current X-Forwarded-For value. Set to a unique IP by the collection prerequest; overridden to 127.0.0.1 by the rate-limiting folder prerequest.
Set by happy-path setup from the gmail_test_email environment variable. Shared across all devices and folders.
Set by happy-path setup. Used for all /login calls in this collection.
Unix timestamp (seconds) recorded in the register prerequest. Used by the Gmail query as after:<ts> to exclude emails from prior runs within the same hour.
6-digit OTP fetched asynchronously from Gmail during the register test script. Consumed by POST /verify-email.
Access token for device A, set after the first /login in happy-path.
Raw refresh_token cookie value for device A, extracted from Set-Cookie after the first /login. Used as the Cookie header in POST /refresh.
Access token for device A after the first /refresh rotation. Used as the Authorization: Bearer header in POST /logout and GET /me.
New refresh_token value for device A after rotation. Used in POST /logout.
Original device A refresh_token before rotation (a copy of sss_refresh_token_a). Kept for reference; the token-reuse chain uses its own dedicated variables.
Access token for device B (sibling session). Set after the second /login in happy-path.
Raw refresh_token cookie value for device B. Used to verify the sibling session survives device A logout.
Access token for device C. Set by the failures folder setup /login.
Raw refresh_token cookie value for device C. Revoked via /logout, then re-presented to /refresh to verify the 401 + cookie-clear behaviour.
Original refresh_token for the token-reuse chain (device D). Set after /login in token-reuse setup. Reused after one rotation to trigger token_reuse_detected.

Successor refresh_token issued after the first /refresh of device D. This is token E — family-killed by RevokeFamilyTokensTx when D is reused. Used in the final token-reuse step to confirm the family kill propagated.
